statement: Iceland implements approximately 75% of EU single market legislation through the EEA Agreement without having any voting rights in the EU institutions that draft and adopt these laws. The EEA Joint Committee, where Iceland has a formal seat, operates by consensus but in practice rubber-stamps EU legislation — Iceland has never exercised its Article 102 'right of reservation' to veto an EEA-relevant act. The EFTA pillar's consultative role is limited to providing input before legislation is finalised, with no amendment power. This arrangement has been described as a 'fax democracy' by Norwegian critics — Iceland receives the laws by fax (metaphorically) and implements them without meaningful influence over their content.
source_name: EEA Agreement, Articles 99–104; EFTA Secretariat — Decision-shaping in the EEA
caveats: The 'fax democracy' characterisation is contested. EFTA states do participate in expert committees and have some influence during the drafting stage. The EEA also excludes many policy areas (agriculture, fisheries, justice, foreign policy) where Iceland retains full sovereignty. The democratic deficit argument works both ways — EU membership would give Iceland a vote but also extend EU law to currently excluded areas.

related_entries.statement: Article 50 of the Treaty on European Union (TEU) provides that any member state may decide to withdraw from the Union in accordance with its own constitutional requirements. The withdrawal process involves a two-year negotiation period, extendable by unanimous agreement of the European Council.

Since the EEA Agreement entered into force in 1994, Iceland has incorporated approximately 13,000 EU legal acts into Icelandic law through the EEA Joint Committee. As of 2025, the EEA acquis covers roughly 75% of the EU single market legislation, excluding agriculture, fisheries, customs union, trade policy, justice and home affairs, foreign policy, and the euro. Iceland has a formal right to veto EEA-relevant legislation through the 'right of reservation' under Article 102 EEA, but this right has never been exercised due to the severe consequences — it would trigger suspension of the affected part of the EEA Agreement.

citations.report_title: Þrír hópar sem myndu hagnast mest á aðild Íslands að Evrópusambandinu

claims.claim_text: Íslendingar hafa lítil sem engin áhrif á mótun þeirra reglna sem gilda á innri markaðnum í gegnum EES-samninginn.

citations.report_title: „Við hefðum náð meiri árangri með því að vera innan Evrópusambandsins“

claims.claim_text: Ísland er eyja og ekki tengt meginlandi Evrópu, sem skapar sérstöðu í samningaviðræðum um ETS-kerfið.
